lesson.title: Integration Techniques
lesson.body: Beyond basic antiderivatives, two key techniques extend our reach. U-substitution reverses the chain rule: substitute u = g(x), du = g'(x)dx to simplify ∫f(g(x))g'(x)dx = ∫f(u)du. Integration by parts reverses the product rule: ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du, where you choose u to differentiate and dv to integrate.

rooms.question: To evaluate ∫(2x)(x²+1)⁵ dx by substitution, set u = ?
rooms.explanation: Set u = x²+1 so du = 2x dx. The integral becomes ∫u⁵ du = u⁶/6 + C = (x²+1)⁶/6 + C.

rooms.question: Evaluate ∫x·eˣ dx using integration by parts with u = x, dv = eˣdx.
rooms.latex: \int x e^x\, dx = xe^x - \int e^x\, dx = xe^x - e^x + C
steps: \int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du = xe^x - e^x + C
rooms.explanation: IBP: ∫x eˣdx = xeˣ − ∫eˣdx = xeˣ − eˣ + C = eˣ(x−1) + C. Both A and D are equivalent.

rooms.question: Evaluate ∫₀¹ 2x·e^{x²} dx.
rooms.explanation: u-sub: u=x², du=2xdx. Limits: u(0)=0, u(1)=1. ∫₀¹ eᵘdu = [eᵘ]₀¹ = e − 1.

rooms.question: Order the u-substitution steps for ∫f(g(x))g'(x) dx:
items: Set u = g(x), compute du = g'(x) dx
Integrate with respect to u: F(u) + C
rooms.explanation: U-substitution: choose substitution, replace, integrate in u, then convert back to x.

rooms.question: The LIATE rule suggests choosing u as the first type in the list. What does LIATE stand for?
answers: Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential
rooms.explanation: LIATE: Logarithm > Inverse trig > Algebraic (polynomial) > Trigonometric > Exponential. Choose u from earlier in the list.

boss.name: The Integration Lich
boss.taunt: Every integral falls to technique. But first you must recognize the pattern.

questions.question: Evaluate ∫ln(x) dx using integration by parts.
questions.latex: \int \ln x\, dx = x\ln x - x + C
questions.explanation: IBP with u = ln x, dv = dx: v = x, du = (1/x)dx. ∫ln x dx = x·ln x − ∫x·(1/x)dx = x ln x − x + C.

questions.question: ∫sin²(x) dx using the identity sin²x = (1 − cos(2x))/2 equals:
questions.explanation: ∫(1−cos2x)/2 dx = x/2 − sin(2x)/4 + C. This is the standard half-angle reduction technique.
